meta.book: Lay, Linear Algebra and Its Applications

items.label: 1a
items.text: Every matrix is row equivalent to a unique matrix in echelon form.

items.label: 1b
items.text: Any system of n linear equations in n variables has at most n solutions.

items.label: 1c
items.text: If a system of linear equations has two different solutions, it must have infinitely many solutions.

items.label: 1d
items.text: If a system of linear equations has no free variables, then it has a unique solution.

items.label: 1e
items.text: If an augmented matrix [A  b] is transformed into [C  d] by elementary row operations, then the equations Ax = b and Cx = d have exactly the same solution sets.

items.label: 1f
items.text: If a system Ax = b has more than one solution, then so does the system Ax = 0.

items.label: 1g
items.text: If A is an m×n matrix and the equation Ax = b is consistent for some b, then the columns of A span R^m.

items.label: 1h
items.text: If an augmented matrix [A  b] can be transformed by elementary row operations into reduced echelon form, then the equation Ax = b is consistent.

items.label: 1i
items.text: If matrices A and B are row equivalent, they have the same reduced echelon form.

items.label: 1j
items.text: The equation Ax = 0 has the trivial solution if and only if there are no free variables.

items.label: 1k
items.text: If A is an m×n matrix and the equation Ax = b is consistent for every b in R^m, then A has m pivot columns.

items.label: 1l
items.text: If an m×n matrix A has a pivot position in every row, then the equation Ax = b has a unique solution for each b in R^m.

items.label: 1m
items.text: If an n×n matrix A has n pivot positions, then the reduced echelon form of A is the n×n identity matrix.

items.label: 1n
items.text: If 3×3 matrices A and B each have three pivot positions, then A can be transformed into B by elementary row operations.

items.label: 1o
items.text: If A is an m×n matrix, if the equation Ax = b has at least two different solutions, and if the equation Ax = c is consistent, then the equation Ax = c has many solutions.

items.label: 1p
items.text: If A and B are row equivalent m×n matrices and if the columns of A span R^m, then so do the columns of B.

items.label: 1q
items.text: If none of the vectors in the set S = {v1, v2, v3} in R^3 is a multiple of one of the other vectors, then S is linearly independent.

items.label: 1r
items.text: If {u, v, w} is linearly independent, then u, v, and w are not in R^2.

items.label: 1s
items.text: In some cases, it is possible for four vectors to span R^5.

items.label: 1t
items.text: If u and v are in R^m, then −u is in Span{u, v}.

items.label: 1u
items.text: If u, v, and w are nonzero vectors in R^2, then w is a linear combination of u and v.

items.label: 1v
items.text: If w is a linear combination of u and v in R^n, then u is a linear combination of v and w.

items.label: 1w
items.text: Suppose that v1, v2, and v3 are in R^5, v2 is not a multiple of v1, and v3 is not a linear combination of v1 and v2. Then {v1, v2, v3} is linearly independent.

items.label: 1x
items.text: A linear transformation is a function.

items.label: 1y
items.text: If A is a 6×5 matrix, the linear transformation x ↦ Ax cannot map R^5 onto R^6.

items.label: 1z
items.text: If A is an m×n matrix with m pivot columns, then the linear transformation x ↦ Ax is a one-to-one mapping.

meta.book: Lay, Linear Algebra and Its Applications

items.label: 1a
items.text: If A and B are m×n, then both AB^T and A^T B are defined.

items.label: 1b
items.text: If AB = C and C has 2 columns, then A has 2 columns.

items.label: 1c
items.text: Left-multiplying a matrix B by a diagonal matrix A, with nonzero entries on the diagonal, scales the rows of B.

items.label: 1d
items.text: If BC = BD, then C = D.

items.label: 1e
items.text: If AC = 0, then either A = 0 or C = 0.

items.label: 1f
items.text: If A and B are n×n, then (A + B)(A − B) = A^2 − B^2.

items.label: 1g
items.text: An elementary n×n matrix has either n or n+1 nonzero entries.

items.label: 1h
items.text: The transpose of an elementary matrix is an elementary matrix.

items.label: 1i
items.text: An elementary matrix must be square.

items.label: 1j
items.text: Every square matrix is a product of elementary matrices.

items.label: 1k
items.text: If A is a 3×3 matrix with three pivot positions, there exist elementary matrices E1,…,Ep such that Ep⋯E1A = I.

items.label: 1l
items.text: If AB = I, then A is invertible.

items.label: 1m
items.text: If A and B are square and invertible, then AB is invertible, and (AB)^-1 = A^-1 B^-1.

items.label: 1n
items.text: If AB = BA and if A is invertible, then A^-1 B = BA^-1.

items.label: 1o
items.text: If A is invertible and if r ≠ 0, then (rA)^-1 = rA^-1.

items.label: 1p
items.text: If A is a 3×3 matrix and the equation Ax = [1, 0, 0]^T has a unique solution, then A is invertible.

meta.book: Lay, Linear Algebra and Its Applications

items.label: 1a
items.text: If A is a 2×2 matrix with a zero determinant, then one column of A is a multiple of the other.

items.label: 1b
items.text: If two rows of a 3×3 matrix A are the same, then det A = 0.

items.label: 1c
items.text: If A is a 3×3 matrix, then det(5A) = 5 det A.

items.label: 1d
items.text: If A and B are n×n matrices, with det A = 2 and det B = 3, then det(A + B) = 5.

items.label: 1e
items.text: If A is n×n and det A = 2, then det A^4 = 6.

items.label: 1f
items.text: If B is produced by interchanging two rows of A, then det B = det A.

items.label: 1g
items.text: If B is produced by multiplying row 3 of A by 5, then det B = 5 det A.

items.label: 1h
items.text: If B is formed by adding to one row of A a linear combination of the other rows, then det B = det A.

items.label: 1i
items.text: det A^T = −det A.

items.label: 1j
items.text: det(−A) = −det A.

items.label: 1k
items.text: det(A^T A) > 0.

items.label: 1l
items.text: Any system of n linear equations in n variables can be solved by Cramer’s rule.

items.label: 1m
items.text: If u and v are in R^2 and det[u v] = 10, then the area of the triangle with vertices 0, u, and v is 10.

items.label: 1n
items.text: If A^2 = 0, then det A = 0.

items.label: 1o
items.text: If A is invertible, then det A^−1 = det A.

items.label: 1p
items.text: If A is invertible, then (det A)(det A^−1) = 1.

meta.book: Lay, Linear Algebra and Its Applications

items.label: 1a
items.text: The set of all linear combinations of v1,…,vp is a vector space.

items.label: 1b
items.text: If {v1,…,vp−1} spans V, then S spans V.

items.label: 1c
items.text: If {v1,…,vp−1} is linearly independent, then so is S.

items.label: 1d
items.text: If S is linearly independent, then S is a basis for V.

items.label: 1e
items.text: If Span S = V, then some subset of S is a basis for V.

items.label: 1f
items.text: If dim V = p and Span S = V, then S cannot be linearly dependent.

items.label: 1g
items.text: A plane in R^3 is a two-dimensional subspace.

items.label: 1h
items.text: The nonpivot columns of a matrix are always linearly dependent.

items.label: 1i
items.text: Row operations on a matrix A can change the linear dependence relations among the rows of A.

items.label: 1j
items.text: Row operations on a matrix can change the null space.

items.label: 1k
items.text: The rank of a matrix equals the number of nonzero rows.

items.label: 1l
items.text: If an m×n matrix A is row equivalent to an echelon matrix U and if U has k nonzero rows, then the dimension of the solution space of Ax = 0 is m − k.

items.label: 1m
items.text: If B is obtained from a matrix A by several elementary row operations, then rank B = rank A.

items.label: 1n
items.text: The nonzero rows of a matrix A form a basis for Row A.

items.label: 1o
items.text: If matrices A and B have the same reduced echelon form, then Row A = Row B.

items.label: 1p
items.text: If H is a subspace of R^3, then there is a 3×3 matrix A such that H = Col A.

items.label: 1q
items.text: If A is m×n and rank A = m, then the linear transformation x ↦ Ax is one-to-one.

items.label: 1r
items.text: If A is m×n and the linear transformation x ↦ Ax is onto, then rank A = m.

items.label: 1s
items.text: A change-of-coordinates matrix is always invertible.

items.label: 1t
items.text: If B = {b1,…,bn} and C = {c1,…,cn} are bases for a vector space V, then the jth column of the change-of-coordinates matrix PC←B is the coordinate vector [cj]B.
